title: The Sowdone of Babylone
summary: The repository XML is copied from University of Michigan CME item CME00053, which encodes only the title page and the 3,274-line poem on pp. 1-94 of Emil Hausknecht's 1881 EETS edition. Hausknecht re-edited the poem directly from its unique Middle English witness, Princeton University Library MS Garrett 140 (formerly Phillipps MS 8357), ff. 1r-41r, while acknowledging and superseding the Roxburghe Club's first printing of 1854. Complete provider objects are cached for Garrett 140, both nineteenth-century editions, a later plate-derived Hausknecht reprint, and Hausknecht's directly consulted source analogue GWLB Ms IV 578. Garrett's full 88-canvas sequence maps the target to ff. 1r-41r, canvases 5-85. Hannover Ms IV 578 has zero target-text overlap: its Destruction de Rome and Fierabras occupy ff. 1r-100v and were consulted only as source analogues. British Library Egerton MS 3028 is another cognate Anglo-Norman witness, but its legacy images were unavailable when checked. The exact Anglo-Norman exemplar or version adapted by the anonymous Middle English poet remains unidentified.
review_notes: The whole-object rule is applied to every downloadable manuscript or edition: cached files retain all provider-exposed covers, endleaves, blanks, adjacent matter, and supporting texts rather than cropping to the poem.

Only Garrett MS 140 contains the Middle English target text. Hannover Ms IV 578 and BL Egerton MS 3028 are source analogues with zero target-text overlap and therefore receive no local-copy work_portion.
The 78 XML manuscript-side milestones reproduce Hausknecht's side/page numbering and are not current Princeton folio numbers; the reliable modern witness locator is ff. 1r-41r.

The cached Toronto scan cataloged as 1881 has an internal Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner title page and a verso noting reprints in 1891 and 1898; it is modeled separately from the original N. Trubner 1881 issue.
Underlying medieval text, editorial text, digital encoding, scanned files, and provider access or reuse terms are separate rights layers.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00053

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item CME00053
entities.description: Public SGML/XML-derived digital text encoding Hausknecht's title page and poem, but not the source volume's introduction, notes, glossary, or index.

entities.label: Hausknecht's 1881 Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone
alternate_labels: The English Charlemagne Romances, Part V
entities.description: The immediate printed source of CME00053, newly edited from the unique late Sir Thomas Phillipps manuscript. The poem is on printed pp. 1-94.

bibliographic.citation: Emil Hausknecht, ed., The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone and of Ferumbras His Sone Who Conquerede Rome, The English Charlemagne Romances, Part V, EETS Extra Series 38 (London: N. Trubner & Co., 1881).
bibliographic.title: The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone and of Ferumbras His Sone Who Conquerede Rome
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 38

entities.label: Post-1898 plate-derived reprint of Hausknecht's edition, cataloged as 1881
entities.description: A later issue preserving the 1881 pagination and text. Its title page names Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co.; the verso says the volume was reprinted in 1891 and 1898, so the scanned issue is not the original N. Trubner 1881 publication despite catalog metadata.

date_statements.label: issue
date_statements.notes: Internal evidence establishes that this is later than the original issue but does not securely identify the exact impression.

bibliographic.citation: Emil Hausknecht, ed., The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone, later Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co. plate-derived issue of EETS Extra Series 38, cataloged as 1881; exact impression unresolved.

entities.label: Roxburghe Club 1854 Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone
entities.description: The first printed edition, copied from Sir Thomas Phillipps's manuscript and collated against the original during printing. The poem occupies printed pp. 1-118 and was printed without modern punctuation.

bibliographic.citation: The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone and of Ferumbras His Sone Who Conquerede Rome (London: printed for the Roxburghe Club by William Nicol, Shakespeare Press, 1854).
bibliographic.title: The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone and of Ferumbras His Sone Who Conquerede Rome
bibliographic.publisher: Roxburghe Club; William Nicol, Shakespeare Press

entities.label: Princeton University Library MS Garrett 140
entities.description: The unique Middle English witness of The Sowdone of Babylone, copied in the East Midlands around 1450. The poem fills ff. 1r-41r; f. 41v carries later ownership material.

holding.notes: Gift of Robert Garrett to Princeton University Library in 1942.

physical_description.format: One column, 38-41 long lines, secretary script
physical_description.dimensions: 268 x 192 mm; text space 200 x 105 mm
notes: Late eighteenth-century quarter-calf binding with marbled-paper pasteboards, rebacked; spine title present.
Unexecuted initials and no decoration; a later-hand line occurs at f. 5r.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Douce 175
entities.description: George Steevens's late-eighteenth-century paper transcript of the unique Sowdone manuscript, given to Francis Douce in 1790. No public digital surrogate was found.

entities.label: British Library Egerton MS 3028
entities.description: A fourteenth-century Anglo-Norman codex containing La Destruccion de Rome at ff. 64r-83r and Fierabras at ff. 84r-118v. These are source analogues, not the Middle English target text, and the codex has zero target-text overlap.

date_statements.label: manuscript production
date_statements.value: second quarter of the fourteenth century

physical_description.extent: 118 leaves plus three front and three rear paper flyleaves
notes: The complete codex also contains an abridged Roman de Brut and continuation at ff. 1r-63r.

entities.label: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz Bibliothek Ms IV 578
entities.description: An Anglo-Norman source-analogue manuscript containing La Destruction de Rome at ff. 1r-24v and Fierabras at ff. 25r-100v. Hausknecht inspected it after drafting his introduction and discussed it in additions on pp. xlix-liv. It does not contain The Sowdone of Babylone and has zero target-text overlap.

notes: Produced in East Anglia; the codex contains 103 miniatures according to scholarly descriptions.

entities.label: Lupack's Three Middle English Charlemagne Romances
entities.description: A later modern edition used for comparison, not an ancestor of CME00053. The 2025 METS digital publication includes an introduction, edited Sowdone text, and notes on PDF pp. 3-89.

bibliographic.citation: Alan Lupack, ed., Three Middle English Charlemagne Romances (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 1990); digital edition, Middle English Texts Series, 2025.
bibliographic.title: Three Middle English Charlemagne Romances

entities.label: Jordi Sanchez-Marti, The Sowdoun of Babyloyne: A Description of the Manuscript
entities.description: A scholarly codicological and provenance description of Garrett MS 140, published in SELIM 12 (2003-2004), pp. 181-189.

bibliographic.citation: Jordi Sanchez-Marti, 'The Sowdoun of Babyloyne: A Description of the Manuscript,' SELIM 12 (2003-2004): 181-189.
bibliographic.title: The Sowdoun of Babyloyne: A Description of the Manuscript

local_copies.label: Complete 210-image GWLB provider sequence
notes: Zero target-text overlap: this manuscript does not contain The Sowdone of Babylone; its source analogues occupy La Destruction de Rome at ff. 1r-24v/canvases 5-52 and Fierabras at ff. 25r-100v/canvases 53-204.

All normal page/surface requests used valid IIIF width syntax 1800,; the narrow spine was retained at its native 620-pixel width and the color target at 1800 x 540. ZIP integrity and representative dimensions were audited.

The provider sequence includes four unlabelled opening surfaces, ff. 1r-100v, four unlabelled closing surfaces, a spine view, and a color target. No separate fore-edge, head-edge, or tail-edge photographs are exposed.

rights.basis: The text was composed and copied in the Middle Ages; U-M marks its CME item No Copyright - United States.
The exact U-M item rights page states that the material is public domain and supplies the No Copyright - United States statement.
notes: U-M frames access as educational and research use; this statement does not establish a license for unrelated General Corpus code or layout.

rights.basis: Published in 1881; the source item is marked not in copyright by Internet Archive metadata.
Internet Archive metadata marks the volume not in copyright, while the embedded Google notice imposes requested file-use conditions and warns that law varies by jurisdiction.
notes: The embedded notice requests personal, noncommercial use, prohibits automated querying, and requests attribution; these file-use terms are distinct from copyright in the 1881 text.

rights.basis: Published in 1854; Internet Archive metadata marks the source item not in copyright.
Internet Archive metadata marks the item not in copyright; the PDF includes a separate Google file-use notice.
notes: Do not infer unrestricted redistribution of the scan file solely from the public-domain status of the 1854 text.

rights.component: Princeton manuscript images and generated PDF
rights.basis: The digital item is labeled Copyright Not Evaluated. The generated PDF says Princeton claims no copyright or license governing the digital resource but frames delivery as free noncommercial open access for fair-use academic and research purposes.
notes: Princeton's current Special Collections policy separately says the Library charges no permission or use fees for publishing collection images and leaves third-party rights clearance to researchers. Seek clarification before redistributing the cached whole facsimile, especially in a repository supporting commercial print-on-demand use.

rights.basis: The official IIIF manifest marks the digital object with the Creative Commons Public Domain Mark 1.0 and supplies attribution text.
notes: Preferred credit: Provided by Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz Bibliothek / No Copyright - Public Domain Marked.

rights.component: 2025 METS digital edition and downloadable PDF
rights.basis: METS states that digital editions are for individual use and directs users to contact its Executive Director before reprinting digital text.
notes: METS separately licenses some XML under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0; that does not automatically license this cached PDF for redistribution.

rights.basis: The institutional repository exposes a public download, but no affirmative redistribution license was established in this review.

editorial_practices.description: U-M encoded the edition title page and poem only, omitting Hausknecht's introduction, notes, glossary, and index.
editorial_practices.scope: Entire CME00053 encoding relative to the complete 1881 volume

editorial_practices.source_feature: The manuscript and 1854 printing lack modern punctuation.
editorial_practices.representation: Hausknecht supplied punctuation in the 1881 reading text.
editorial_practices.description: Hausknecht explicitly added punctuation absent from both the manuscript and the 1854 first edition.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript abbreviations and abbreviated letter sequences
editorial_practices.representation: The XML contains 327 italic runs, many isolating supplied expansion letters, but the edition does not provide a global rule proving that every italic run is an expansion.
editorial_practices.description: Italic type often appears to distinguish supplied letters in expanded forms; individual cases should be compared with the manuscript rather than generalized without qualification.
editorial_practices.scope: Edited poem and its CME00053 encoding

editorial_practices.source_feature: Unique manuscript, scribal blunders, and differences from the 1854 printing
editorial_practices.representation: Slight deviations and important corrections are documented in footnotes or end notes; word division and hyphenation are editorialized.
editorial_practices.description: Hausknecht reports separating some manuscript-joined words, hyphenating some manuscript-separated forms, footnoting deviations, and correcting important scribal errors; Herrtage checked a transcript against the manuscript.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00053.xml, pinned commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Identifies the U-M item, Hausknecht edition, encoded title page and poem, 3,274 verse lines, print-page milestones 1-94, manuscript-side milestones, italics, abbreviation markup, notes, and 2016 review metadata.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00053 full text.
evidence.summary: Confirms public access, discovery metadata, Hausknecht's 1881 source, printed pages 1-94, and the 3,274-line encoded poem.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan, Rights and Permissions for CME00053.
evidence.summary: The exact item rights statement identifies the material as public domain and links No Copyright - United States while describing educational and research access.
notes: The U-M interface's rights link was reviewed on the exact item; URL routing may resolve through the item page.

evidence.citation: Emil Hausknecht, ed., The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone, EETS Extra Series 38 (London: N. Trubner & Co., 1881), complete original-issue scan.
evidence.locator: title page; pp. xlvii-liv; poem pp. 1-94; notes pp. 95-112
evidence.summary: Directly establishes the original issue, manuscript basis, relationship to the 1854 edition, punctuation and word-division practices, collation and corrections, poem pagination, and Hausknecht's consultation of Hannover Ms IV 578.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive item romauncesowdone00hausgoog.
evidence.summary: Provides the original-issue complete scan, OCR and structural sidecars, item metadata, and public-domain/copyright metadata.

evidence.citation: Internal title page and reprint-history verso of Internet Archive item romaunceofsono3800hausuoft.
evidence.locator: title page and verso; poem PDF pp. 77-170
evidence.summary: Shows Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co. and the statement '[Reprinted 1891, 1898.]', proving that the scanned item is a later plate-derived issue rather than the original N. Trubner 1881 issue.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive item romaunceofsono3800hausuoft.
evidence.summary: Catalogs the scanned volume as 1881 and exposes its complete PDF and sidecars; the internal issue evidence is more specific than the catalog date.

evidence.citation: The Romaunce of the Sowdone of Babylone (Roxburghe Club, 1854), complete scan.
evidence.summary: Establishes the first edition, its copying from and during-printing collation against Sir Thomas Phillipps's manuscript, its complete poem extent, and absence of editorial punctuation.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive item romauncesowdone00clubgoog.
evidence.summary: Provides the complete 1854 scan, OCR and structural sidecars, and not-in-copyright metadata.

evidence.citation: Princeton University Library catalog record 99108870093506421, [Sowdon of Babyloyne].
evidence.summary: Authoritative current shelfmark, date, East Midlands origin, unique-work foliation ff. 1r-41r, dimensions, layout, script, binding, ownership history, and 1942 gift provenance.

evidence.citation: Princeton Digital Collections, Sowdon of Babyloyne, Garrett MS 140.
evidence.summary: Official digital-object page, metadata, Copyright Not Evaluated statement, and routes to the full digitization.

evidence.citation: Princeton Figgy IIIF Presentation manifest for Garrett MS 140.
evidence.summary: Enumerates all 88 canvases and their labels, establishing the complete surface sequence and the exact target mapping at canvases 5-85.

evidence.citation: Princeton-generated complete PDF for Garrett MS 140, rights/title page.
evidence.summary: States Copyright Not Evaluated, says Princeton claims no copyright or license governing the resource, and frames provision as free noncommercial open access for fair-use academic and research purposes.

evidence.citation: Princeton University Library, Special Collections Copyright, Credit, and Citation Guidelines.
evidence.summary: States that Princeton charges no permission or use fees for publication of collection images, asks for credit, and leaves third-party rights compliance to researchers.

evidence.citation: Jordi Sanchez-Marti, 'The Sowdoun of Babyloyne: A Description of the Manuscript,' SELIM 12 (2003-2004): 181-189.
evidence.summary: Supports unique-witness status, former Phillipps 8357 and Heber 1533 designations, provenance, collation and physical description, approximate composition around 1400, and a cautious lost/unknown Anglo-Norman source relationship.
notes: The article reports 275 x 203 mm and 39-43 lines; current Princeton catalog measurements of 268 x 192 mm and 38-41 lines are preferred for current descriptive data while the conflict is retained.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Princeton University Library MS Garrett 140.
evidence.summary: Confirms circa-1450 East Midlands production, parchment support, unique copy at ff. 1r-41r, 3,274 lines, and IMEV 950.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, The Sultan of Babylon.
evidence.summary: Supports late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century composition, East Midlands origin, stanza form, IMEV 950, plot and motifs, unique Garrett witness, and the Hausknecht and Lupack editions.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MARCO record for MS. Douce 175.
evidence.summary: Identifies the 104-page paper transcript made by George Steevens from the Sowdone manuscript and given to Douce in 1790; no public facsimile is linked.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Egerton MS 3028.
evidence.summary: Gives date, origin, support, extent, dimensions, full contents and folio ranges; reports legacy full digital coverage but states that digital images are currently unavailable and links a request form.

evidence.citation: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz Bibliothek digital collections, Ms IV 578.
evidence.summary: Official object record and persistent access route for the complete 100-leaf manuscript, its Anglo-Norman contents, physical description, images, and PDF.

evidence.citation: GWLB IIIF Presentation manifest for Ms IV 578.
evidence.summary: Enumerates the complete 210-image provider sequence, direct image services, Public Domain Mark 1.0, and requested attribution.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Alan Lupack, ed., Three Middle English Charlemagne Romances.
evidence.summary: Official publication record, introduction and bibliography for Lupack's 1990 edition as digitally published in 2025; identifies the later edited text and manuscript basis.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Copyright and Citation.
evidence.summary: States individual-use limits for digital editions and directs users to seek permission before reprinting digital text; separately identifies terms for XML.

evidence.citation: Local checksum, file-count, image-dimension, PDF-page-count, ZIP-integrity, and hard-link audit of source-cache/CME00053.
evidence.summary: Verified 19 cached files, exact checksums and byte sizes, true hard links between the canonical main-repository cache and the research worktree, Princeton's complete 88-image/89-PDF-page sequence, GWLB's complete 210-image/210-PDF-page sequence, valid 1800, IIIF width requests, and ZIP integrity.

open_questions.question: Which lost or surviving Anglo-Norman version most directly underlies the Middle English poem?
next_steps: Treat Egerton MS 3028 and Hannover Ms IV 578 only as cognate source witnesses unless a stemmatic study identifies a direct exemplar.
Review recent source studies of La Destruction de Rome and Fierabras before assigning a more specific adaptation relationship.

open_questions.question: How should Princeton's generated-PDF noncommercial/fair-use wording be reconciled with its current no-permission-fee publication policy for collection images?
next_steps: Ask Princeton Special Collections whether the complete cached facsimile may be redistributed in a public Git repository that can support commercial print-on-demand projects.
Until clarified, link to Princeton's official facsimile and use local files for research rather than publishing them.

open_questions.question: When will the British Library's legacy full digitization of Egerton MS 3028 become publicly accessible again, and is a whole-object download available by request?
next_steps: Submit the official digitized-manuscript request form, quoting Egerton MS 3028 and asking for the whole physical object rather than selected folios.

open_questions.question: Does every italic run in Hausknecht and CME00053 represent supplied expansion letters, or do italics serve additional functions?
next_steps: Compare a stratified sample of all 327 HI italic runs with Garrett MS 140 and Hausknecht's footnotes before encoding a global rendering rule.

open_questions.question: What exact impression and year does the Toronto-scanned post-1898 plate-derived Hausknecht issue represent?
next_steps: Compare publisher imprints and EETS issue records for the 1891, 1898, and later impressions; retain the original 1881 scan as the immediate edition surrogate.

open_questions.question: Why do Sanchez-Marti's measurements and line counts differ from Princeton's current catalog?
next_steps: Prefer current Princeton catalog data for display, preserve the attributed scholarly variant, and ask the repository only if exact codicological measurement is material.
